multi_tenant_overhead.key_finding: Multi-tenant adds 4.48% more Netty-IO CPU (47.03% vs 42.55%), primarily from HPACK table operations per tenant. HpackDecoder.decode jumps from 1.38% to 3.33%.

improvement_queue.title: Optimize Netty HTTP/2 header processing (HPACK decode/encode, DefaultHeaders)
improvement_queue.description: 42.55% of CPU is in Netty I/O. Top methods: String.hashCode (3.18%), DefaultHeaders$HeaderEntry.pointNeighborsToThis (1.94%), HpackDecoder.decode (1.38%). Alloc: DefaultHeaders.newHeaderEntry (3.38%), Http2HeaderEntry (2.68%), HpackDecoder.readStringLiteral (2.24%). Consider: reduce header copies, cache/pool header objects, avoid repeated String.hashCode on same keys, reduce DefaultHeaders bucket resizing.

improvement_queue.title: Reduce byte[]/String allocation and copying
improvement_queue.description: byte[] is 15.16% of all allocations (52 GB sampled). String is 3.35%, plus copyOfRange 3.14%, copyOf 2.04%. Key sites: Arrays.copyOfRangeByte (3.14%), Arrays.copyOf (2.04%), StringUTF16.compress (1.24%). Consider: buffer pooling, avoid intermediate byte[] copies, use direct ByteBuf reads.

improvement_queue.title: Reduce HashMap/collection allocation overhead
improvement_queue.description: 14.67% of allocations are collections. HashMap.newNode (6.29%), HashMap.resize (2.70%), HashMap (1.22%). Pre-size HashMaps, reduce rehashing, pool per-request maps, use HashMap.putIfAbsent. Also: String.hashCode is #1 CPU method (3.18%) from heavy HashMap use.

improvement_queue.title: Reduce Reactor operator chain overhead and allocations
improvement_queue.description: 11.92% CPU + 16.54% alloc in Reactor. FluxOnErrorResume$ResumeSubscriber (1.07%), FluxMap$MapSubscriber (1.02%), HttpClientConnect.duplicate (1.30% alloc). Flatten operator chains, reduce intermediate mono/flux wrapping, use Mono.defer less, consider operator fusion.

improvement_queue.title: Optimize SDK internal request/response processing
improvement_queue.description: 7.73% CPU in SDK code. AbstractListDecorator.decorate (0.98%), HttpHeaders.set (1.16% alloc), HttpHeader allocation (1.16% alloc). Optimize internal header handling, reduce list decoration overhead, cache computed values.

improvement_queue.title: Optimize JSON serialization/deserialization
improvement_queue.description: 4.41% CPU + 4.23% alloc in JSON processing. Consider: pre-compiled serializers, object reuse, streaming API, avoid intermediate tree model.

improvement_queue.title: Reduce regex usage in hot paths
improvement_queue.description: 3.55% CPU in regex. Pattern$GroupTail.match (0.98%), URI$Parser.scan (0.86%), URI$Parser.parseHierarchical (1.23% in 30t). Replace regex with manual string operations in hot paths, pre-compile patterns, use indexOf/substring instead.

improvement_queue.title: Reduce per-tenant HPACK table overhead for multi-tenant scenarios
improvement_queue.description: Multi-tenant adds 4.48% more Netty-IO CPU. HpackDecoder.decode jumps from 1.38% to 3.33%, HpackDynamicTable.add appears at 1.91%, HpackEncoder.getEntryInsensitive at 1.30%. Per-tenant connection pools create separate HPACK compression contexts. Consider: connection sharing, HPACK table size tuning, shared static header tables.

iteration_log.name: netty-http2-header-processing
iteration_log.hypothesis: Optimizing HTTP/2 header encoding/decoding in the Netty transport layer can reduce CPU overhead during request serialization, improving throughput especially at higher concurrency levels where header processing becomes a bottleneck.
iteration_log.approach_summary: Optimize HPACK header compression and AsciiString handling in the Cosmos DB HTTP/2 transport pipeline to reduce per-request CPU cost.
iteration_log.failure_reason: Catastrophic regressions across multiple configs. 1t-c32 ReadThroughput regressed -7.77% (confirmed with 0.23% stddev). 30t-c3 WriteThroughput regressed -48.47% (significant). 30t-c3 ReadThroughput showed extreme instability (41.13% stddev) suggesting JIT deoptimization or lock contention introduced by the changes. p99 latency regressed >20% at 3 test points. Early stop triggered after detecting 2 significant regressions; 30t-c5 was skipped. No statistically significant improvements were observed.
iteration_log.lessons_learned: The Netty HTTP/2 header processing path is deeply integrated with Netty's internal data structures (DefaultHeaders, HpackDecoder/Encoder). Attempting to optimize this at the SDK layer without modifying Netty internals is extremely risky. The changes likely introduced overhead in the multi-threaded paths (30t configs showed catastrophic degradation) â€” possibly due to added synchronization, extra object allocation, or disruption of Netty's carefully optimized header hash table structure. The 1t-c32 Read regression (-7.77% with only 0.23% stddev) suggests a deterministic performance penalty in the hot path. The 30t-c3 instability (one rep at 16K vs two at 38K) points to intermittent lock contention or JIT deoptimization.
iteration_log.future_guidance: Do NOT attempt to intercept or modify Netty's internal header processing (DefaultHeaders, HpackDecoder, HpackEncoder) from the SDK layer. These are highly optimized with careful cache-line and hash-bucket sizing. Instead, focus on: (1) reducing the NUMBER of headers sent/received (skip unnecessary custom headers), (2) caching parsed header values at the Cosmos SDK level to avoid re-parsing, (3) using Netty's built-in header pooling/caching mechanisms rather than replacing them. The 30t-c3 config is particularly sensitive to contention â€” any optimization touching shared header state must be tested at multi-thread configs first.

iteration_log.name: hashmap-collection-allocation
iteration_log.hypothesis: Reducing HashMap/collection allocation overhead in the gateway response path eliminates per-response intermediate HashMap creation (currently ~10% of all allocations from HashMap.newNode + HashMap.resize + HashMap objects). Pre-sizing HashMaps on the request path avoids resize/rehash operations. These changes should reduce GC pressure and CPU overhead from HashMap operations (String.hashCode is 3.18% CPU, HashMap.putVal is 1.30% CPU), improving throughput especially at high concurrency.
iteration_log.approach_summary: 1) Added new StoreResponse constructor accepting HttpHeaders directly â€” populates String[] arrays without intermediate HashMap via new HttpHeaders.populateLowerCaseHeaders() method. 2) Updated JsonNodeStorePayload with array-based constructor that builds Map lazily only on error paths. 3) Pre-sized HashMaps: HttpHeaders request construction (defaultHeaders.size + headers.size), StoreResponse.replicaStatusList(4), withRemappedStatusCode(headerCount), RxDocumentServiceRequest fallback(32). 4) Fixed HttpUtils.asMap() double-allocation by iterating HttpHeaders directly instead of via toMap().

hotspot_changes.reason: JFR not captured - benchmarks were already running when agent started. However, the optimization approach (eliminating intermediate HashMap allocations, pre-sizing collections, removing double-allocation in HttpUtils.asMap()) clearly eliminates work rather than moving it. The +14.8% improvement at 1t-c128 with 20% less CPU (91.9% â†’ 73%) confirms reduced per-operation overhead.

iteration_log.lessons_learned: HashMap/collection allocation optimization is highly effective for the Cosmos SDK gateway path. Key wins: (1) Eliminating intermediate HashMap in StoreResponse construction provides the largest gain â€” the per-response HashMap creation + population + iteration was pure waste. (2) Pre-sizing HashMaps avoids resize/rehash cascades that compound under concurrency. (3) Read workloads benefit far more than Write workloads (avg +13.7% vs +3.7%) because reads have lighter server-side processing, making client-side overhead proportionally larger. (4) The 1t-c128 config showed the largest Read gain (+14.8%) with dramatic CPU reduction (92% â†’ 73%), confirming that HashMap allocation/GC was a major CPU consumer at high concurrency. (5) The relatively flat Write improvement suggests Write bottleneck is server-side, not client-side collection allocation.
iteration_log.future_guidance: The HashMap optimization pattern is proven and safe â€” further collection-related improvements should follow the same approach: (1) identify per-request HashMap/ArrayList creation, (2) replace with direct array population or pre-sized collections, (3) defer Map construction to error paths only. The 1t-c128 Write config (-0.1%) is the only neutral point â€” Write at high concurrency is server-bound, so don't expect further client-side gains there. Future optimizations in the response path should build on this branch and preserve the direct String[] array population pattern in StoreResponse. The lazy Map in JsonNodeStorePayload is safe because error paths are rare (<0.1% of requests).

iteration_log.name: reactor-pipeline-overhead
iteration_log.hypothesis: Reducing Reactor operator chain overhead by eliminating redundant operators (.single()), merging consecutive .map() calls, pre-applying constant HttpClient configuration at construction (avoiding per-request HttpClientConfig duplication), and inlining session capture will lower per-request allocation count by ~7 objects and shorten the signal propagation path from ~39 to ~34 operators. This reduces GC pressure and CPU overhead from subscriber creation/traversal, improving throughput especially at high concurrency where allocation rate is highest.
iteration_log.approach_summary: Five targeted changes: (1) Pre-apply constant HttpClient settings (keepAlive, connect timeout) at construction instead of per-request â€” saves 2 reactor-netty duplicate() calls per request. (2) Replace .single() with .next() in ReactorNettyClient.send() â€” lighter operator for guaranteed single-element Flux. (3) Remove redundant .single() in RxGatewayStoreModel (body.aggregate returns Mono, invokeAsyncInternal returns Mono). (4) Merge consecutive .map() operators for StoreResponse+RxDocumentServiceResponse into single .map(). (5) Inline session capture in processMessage â€” synchronous captureSessionToken() on common path, async only for rare partition split.
iteration_log.failure_reason: Zero incremental throughput improvement over the hashmap-collection-allocation base branch. All 10 workload/config combinations produced identical average ops/s (0.0% delta at every config). The minimum requirement of â‰¥1% improvement at â‰¥2 configs was not met at ANY config. Additionally, the reactor candidate showed significantly increased throughput variance at multi-tenant Read configs: 30t-c3 Read stddev rose from 1.6% to 18.6%, 30t-c5 Read from 1.8% to 14.5%, and 1t-c1 Read from 3.6% to 10.1% â€” suggesting potential JIT instability introduced by the operator chain modifications. The identical throughput averages (to the decimal) across all configs also raises the possibility that the reactor code changes were not included in the benchmark JAR, though some per-rep distributions differ (confirming separate benchmark runs that simply converged to the same mean).
iteration_log.lessons_learned: Reactor operator chain micro-optimizations (removing .single(), merging .map(), inlining session capture) have negligible throughput impact in the Cosmos DB gateway path. The Reactor overhead (~12% CPU, ~17% alloc) is distributed across many small operators â€” removing 5-7 of ~39 operators saves ~0.5% allocation, which is below the measurement noise floor. The per-request HttpClientConfig.duplicate() optimization also had no measurable effect, suggesting reactor-netty's clone operation is already highly optimized (likely a simple field copy). Key insight: Reactor overhead is a 'death by a thousand cuts' problem â€” individual operators are too cheap to optimize individually. A meaningful improvement would require a fundamentally different approach (e.g., operator fusion, custom Subscriber that bypasses the chain for the common path). The increased variance at multi-tenant configs (30t-c3/c5 Read stddev >14%) warrants investigation â€” it may indicate the .single() â†’ .next() replacement removes a stabilizing synchronization point.
iteration_log.future_guidance: Do NOT pursue incremental Reactor operator removal for the gateway path â€” the per-operator overhead is below the noise floor (~0.1% each). Instead, for meaningful Reactor gains: (1) Investigate operator fusion (Reactor's built-in Fuseable interface) to batch multiple operators into single passes. (2) Consider a custom 'fast path' Subscriber for the common gateway requestâ†’response flow that short-circuits the full operator chain. (3) The HttpClientConfig.duplicate() path is NOT worth optimizing further â€” reactor-netty's clone is already cheap. (4) The increased stddev at 30t Read configs suggests the .single() operator was providing implicit backpressure or synchronization â€” future Reactor changes must be tested at 30t configs to verify stability. (5) Reactor's allocation overhead is dominated by Subscriber objects â€” if pursuing allocation reduction, target the Subscriber creation pattern rather than the operator count.

iteration_log.hypothesis: Eliminating the 4-level vendored UnmodifiableList delegation chain in LocationCache, replacing HttpHeaders internal Map<String,HttpHeader> with Map<String,String>, and using setLowered() in ReactorNettyClient to skip redundant toLowerCase() would save ~1.5% CPU and ~1% allocation for a 2-3% throughput improvement.
iteration_log.name: sdk-internal-processing
iteration_log.lessons_learned: Three key lessons: (1) Changing HttpHeaders internal storage from Map<String,HttpHeader> to Map<String,String> increased HashMap.putVal/getNode pressure -- the HttpHeader object served as a single allocation that the JIT could track, while raw String approach multiplied map operations. (2) setLowered() did NOT eliminate toLowerCase -- StringLatin1.toLowerCase actually increased +2.65% in multi-tenant configs, suggesting either the code path wasn't reached (HTTP/1.1 fallback?) or other callers invoke toLowerCase. (3) Changes disrupted Netty's internal pipeline: new hotspots in HttpObjectDecoder.splitHeader (+1.91%) and ByteBufUtil.firstIndexOf (+3.01%) suggest the header processing changes caused Netty to use a different codec path. Inconsistent per-config results (wins at c32/c3, losses at c1/c5) suggest optimization only helps at specific concurrency levels.
iteration_log.approach_summary: Three changes: (1) Replaced vendored Apache Commons UnmodifiableList with Collections.unmodifiableList() in LocationCache.DatabaseAccountLocationsInfo and related methods. (2) Changed HttpHeaders internal storage from Map<String,HttpHeader> to Map<String,String>, adding setLowered()/valueLowered() methods that skip toLowerCase(). HttpHeader objects created lazily in iterator(). (3) Used setLowered() in ReactorNettyClient.ReactorNettyHttpResponse.headers() lambda to avoid toLowerCase on HTTP/2 headers that are protocol-guaranteed lowercase.
iteration_log.failure_reason: Failed on 4 of 5 acceptance criteria: (1) Only 2 configs show >=2% avg throughput improvement (need 3). (2) Throughput regressions of -7.4% (1t-c1 Read) and -9.5% (30t-c5 Read) exceed 5% max regression limit. (3) p99 latency regressions of +16.8% (1t-c1 Read), +10.1% (30t-c3 Write), +26.5% (30t-c5 Read) exceed 10% limit. (4) JFR shows work moved to HashMap.putVal/getNode and StringLatin1.toLowerCase, not eliminated. 30t-c3 Read shows 17.2% CV (JIT fragility).
iteration_log.future_guidance: For HttpHeaders optimization: (1) Do NOT change Map<String,HttpHeader> representation -- HttpHeader wrapper provides JIT-stable optimization target. Use CaseInsensitiveMap or AsciiString keys instead. (2) Verify whether ReactorNettyClient uses HTTP/2 in all benchmark configs or falls back to HTTP/1.1. (3) LocationCache UnmodifiableList change should be tested in isolation. (4) Future attempts should split into separate PRs tested independently. (5) 1t-c1 config is highly sensitive to per-request overhead changes.

hotspot_changes.assessment: Work was shifted, not eliminated. HashMap operations (putVal +2.75%, getNode +1.59%) increased significantly. StringLatin1.toLowerCase appeared at +2.65% in 30t-c3, contradicting the hypothesis. HttpObjectDecoder methods appeared, suggesting disrupted Netty HTTP/2 pipeline optimization.

current_improvement.name: sdk-internal-processing
current_improvement.hypothesis: Two isolated, targeted changes can reduce SDK internal processing overhead without disrupting Netty pipelines or changing the JIT-stable Map<String,HttpHeader> representation (which caused regressions in attempt 1). (1) Replacing the vendored UnmodifiableList 4-level virtual dispatch chain with JDK Collections.unmodifiableList() eliminates ~1.02% CPU from AbstractListDecorator.decorated() on every per-request endpoint resolution. (2) Adding an isAsciiLowerCase fast-path in HttpHeaders.set()/value() avoids the full StringLatin1.toLowerCase machinery for already-lowercase header keys (HTTP/2 response headers, x-ms-* custom headers), saving ~1.29% CPU. Combined ~2.3% CPU reduction should yield 2-4% throughput improvement at CPU-bound configs.
current_improvement.approach:
Two independent, safely isolated changes:

Change 1: LocationCache UnmodifiableList elimination
- Replace all UnmodifiableList<T> fields in DatabaseAccountLocationsInfo with List<T>
- Use Collections.unmodifiableList() instead of new UnmodifiableList<>() throughout LocationCache
- Update return types in LocationCache and GlobalEndpointManager from UnmodifiableList<T> to List<T>
- Update variable declarations in ClientRetryPolicy and GlobalPartitionEndpointManagerForPerPartitionCircuitBreaker
- JFR evidence: AbstractListDecorator.decorated() is 1.02% CPU (398 samples / 38848 total at 1t-c128). The decorated() call traverses 3 virtual method calls + 1 cast per list operation.

Change 2: HttpHeaders toLowerCase fast-path
- Add private static isAsciiLowerCase(String) method to HttpHeaders: simple char range check (c >= A && c <= Z) that short-circuits on first uppercase char
- Use in set(): String headerKey = isAsciiLowerCase(name) ? name : name.toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT)
- Use in value() and getHeader(): same fast-path
- CRITICAL: Keep Map<String, HttpHeader> representation unchanged
- CRITICAL: Do NOT add setLowered()/valueLowered() public API
- JFR evidence: StringLatin1.toLowerCase is 1.29% CPU (500 samples).

What this does NOT do (lessons from attempt 1):
- Does NOT change Map<String,HttpHeader> to Map<String,String>
- Does NOT add setLowered() public API
- Does NOT touch ReactorNettyClient or Netty pipeline
- Does NOT bundle risky changes
